evidence: README.md defines governance that contract surfaces must be expressed through shared typed APIs and shared registry files, spanning protocol actions and StarTram error contracts.
goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go defines `protocolUploadResetContract` with `Namespace: protocolUploadOpenEndpointNamespace`, coupling reset to a constant named for a different action.

goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go duplicates lifecycle policy values through two constant layers (`protocol*IntroducedIn`/`protocol*Compatibility` and `uploadAction*`/`c2c*` aliases), increasing drift surface in contract metadata management.

goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go repeats the same per-entry metadata pattern as protocol contracts, reinforcing per-file contract metadata ownership instead of a single canonical policy layer.

goseg/importer/importer.go defines importerRuntime with 7 nested runtime-op groups and then re-exposes many pass-through methods (e.g., storagePathFor/swapSettings/.../createDir) that mostly nil-check and forward calls.

goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go introduces multiple callback-bag interfaces (InitPhase, BackgroundServiceGroup, ServerBootstrap) and adapter structs where methods primarily assemble slices or forward callbacks.

goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go duplicates near-identical container runtime constructors (defaultRuntimeContainerOps and defaultRuntimeContainerOpsWithLifecycle), indicating indirection variants without behavioral differentiation.

goseg/main.go and goseg/rectify/rectify.go add additional runtime wrapper layers (bootstrapRuntime/bootstrapRuntimeFns and NewRectifyRuntime/resolveRectifyRuntime) that increase call depth for default behavior wiring.

Runtime dependency assembly is duplicated instead of composed: `defaultRuntimeContainerOps` and `defaultRuntimeContainerOpsWithLifecycle` are effectively identical constructor blocks (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:342`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:359`).

Config patching relies on string field names and reflection (`patchField: "..."` + `FieldByName`) across registry/schema/model layers, creating a brittle multi-file convention rather than typed linkage (`goseg/config/config_update_registry.go:245`, `goseg/config/config_update_registry.go:163`, `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go:88`, `goseg/structs/configs.go:88`).

Workflow handlers combine orchestration, persistence, dispatch, and transition event semantics in single functions, making behavior hard to evolve in isolation (`goseg/shipworkflow/startram_lifecycle_runtime.go:69`, `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go:118`).

Large test files aggregate many behavioral domains and infrastructure seams into single modules, reducing locality and making responsibility boundaries unclear (`goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go:16`, `goseg/importer/importer_test.go:183`).

Session state management is split into two ad-hoc stores with overlapping bucket/list mutation logic and custom lifecycle rules rather than a shared model (`goseg/session/connection_registry.go:106`, `goseg/session/logstream_session_store.go:167`).

findings.summary: Package-global mutable contract catalogs create hidden coupling across contract files.
evidence: `protocolContractCatalogSpecs` and `startramContractCatalogSpecs` are declared as `var` slices at package scope rather than immutable values.
Entry/snapshot functions in both files read those globals, so same-package code can mutate registry content without API-level controls.
findings.suggestion: Make catalog specs effectively immutable: store as fixed arrays or unexported constants of structs where possible, expose read-only accessors that always return copies, and centralize catalog assembly in one registry builder file to prevent per-file mutable globals.

findings.summary: Importer runtime uses a deep pass-through seam stack where forwarding code outweighs domain behavior.
evidence: importerRuntime composes RuntimeTransitionOps plus config/workflow/lifecycle/shipConfig/container/volume/files runtime bags and then defines many wrappers that mostly check nil and forward (e.g., lines ~593-825).
withDefaultsImporterRuntime manually merges each runtime bag field-by-field, adding another configuration layer before real importer workflow logic executes.
shipworkflow and orchestration already provide runtime seam bundles, so importer adds a second orchestration seam layer rather than consuming a narrower workflow-focused contract.
findings.suggestion: Replace importerRuntime’s multi-bag surface with 2-3 cohesive capability interfaces aligned to importer phases (init, upload-io, ship-provisioning). Inject those directly and remove the forwarding methods that only nil-check and delegate.

findings.summary: Startup orchestration models callback groups as interfaces/adapter structs with little polymorphic value.
evidence: InitPhase/BackgroundServiceGroup/ServerBootstrap each have runtime structs whose methods mostly build static slices or forward single callbacks (e.g., SubsystemSteps, ServiceSpecs, StartConfigEventLoop).

Tests primarily instantiate concrete runtime structs/pointers directly (startup_orchestrator_test), indicating interface indirection is used mainly as callback packaging rather than true substitutable behavior.
main.go still passes empty runtime wrappers (bootstrapRuntimeWith(nil,nil,nil), StartupRuntime{}), so startup path traverses abstractions even when defaults are always intended.
findings.suggestion: Flatten startup runtime wiring into explicit plan structs (initSteps/backgroundSpecs/bootstrapFns) and eliminate interface adapters unless multiple production implementations exist. Keep only function fields needed for tests.

findings.summary: Two container runtime constructor variants carry the same implementation, creating a false abstraction split.
evidence: defaultRuntimeContainerOps and defaultRuntimeContainerOpsWithLifecycle in runtime_ops.go are effectively identical (same function assignments and fields).
Callers choose between them by name (e.g., defaultRuntimeTransitionOps uses *WithLifecycle), but no lifecycle-specific behavior exists in the selected variant.
findings.suggestion: Collapse both constructors into one canonical builder and, if lifecycle-specific behavior is needed later, add explicit options or decorator functions instead of duplicate named constructors.

findings.summary: Repeated default/override resolution helpers create low-leverage indirection across subsystems.
evidence: main.go uses bootstrapRuntime interface + bootstrapRuntimeFns + bootstrapRuntimeWith just to provide optional overrides; production main path still calls bootstrapRuntimeWith(nil,nil,nil).
rectify.go has newRectifyRuntime/NewRectifyRuntime/NewRectifyRuntimeWithDependencies/resolveRectifyRuntime layering where most calls resolve back to the same default runtime.
wifi_runtime_api.go has both resolveWiFiRuntime and duplicated defaulting logic in NewWiFiRuntimeService, repeating runtime-resolution patterns rather than sharing one constructor path.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one runtime-construction pattern (single constructor + optional options struct) and remove parallel resolve/with/default helpers that only re-route to defaults.

findings.summary: Upload reset contract is bound via an open-endpoint-named namespace constant.
evidence: `protocolUploadResetContract` sets `Namespace: protocolUploadOpenEndpointNamespace` rather than a reset-agnostic/upload namespace symbol.
README governance text emphasizes explicit typed contract boundaries; this naming-level coupling weakens that boundary by encoding one action name into another action's binding.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a neutral upload namespace constant (for example `protocolUploadNamespace`) and bind both upload actions to it; avoid cross-action-named constants in contract entries.

findings.summary: Contract compatibility/version policy is duplicated across family files instead of one canonical policy layer.
evidence: Protocol contracts define per-entry lifecycle constants and then re-alias them again (`protocol...IntroducedIn` plus `uploadAction...IntroducedIn`, same for compatibility), creating multiple write points for one policy fact.
StarTram contracts use a separate per-file constant set for equivalent metadata shape, repeating policy representation in another family file.

README declares cross-family governance for protocol/error contract surfaces, but metadata remains managed in scattered per-family constants, which increases docs-vs-runtime drift risk as families evolve.
findings.suggestion: Refactor contract family files to consume a single canonical metadata declaration path (for example one registry-centered constructor/helper in `contracts.go`) and remove alias-layer duplication so each contract policy fact is declared once.

findings.summary: Runtime seam construction duplicates large constructor blocks, increasing drift risk and hiding true variation points.
evidence: `defaultRuntimeContainerOps` and `defaultRuntimeContainerOpsWithLifecycle` return the same field assignments and both allocate a new network runtime (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go:342-373`).

Tests in startup orchestration validate default wiring behavior, which means duplicated constructors become parallel maintenance surfaces for the same runtime composition intent (`goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go:225`).
findings.suggestion: Collapse container runtime defaults into a single constructor and make any lifecycle-specific difference explicit via parameters/options; keep one authoritative seam assembly path and test that path directly.

findings.summary: Config patch application depends on string-key and reflection conventions across files instead of typed bindings.
evidence: Registry entries repeat `patchField` strings (for example `"GSVersion"`, `"CfgDir"`) and runtime lookup uses `reflect.Value.Elem().FieldByName(field)` (`goseg/config/config_update_registry.go:383`, `goseg/config/config_update_registry.go:163`).

Schema carries the same `patchField` indirection in `confPatchField`, while target structs define actual fields separately (`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go:88`, `goseg/structs/configs.go:99-101`).

Because matching is name-based and runtime-checked, rename/refactor safety is weak and failures can become silent no-ops depending on missing fields (`goseg/config/config_update_registry.go:174-177`, `goseg/config/config_update_registry.go:194-195`).
findings.suggestion: Replace reflective field-name routing with typed accessors for every patch field (or generated binding table) so compile-time checks enforce mapping correctness between patch schema and config structs.

findings.summary: Ship workflow handlers combine state transition design, side-effect orchestration, and persistence logic in single bodies.
evidence: `runStartramToggleWithRuntime` both computes transition steps and executes config writes, container operations, per-pier dispatches, and MinIO rebuild sequencing (`goseg/shipworkflow/startram_lifecycle_runtime.go:69-143`).

`buildMinIODomainSteps` and `buildUrbitDomainSteps` duplicate alias validation + mutation flow patterns while embedding external API calls and config writes in step lambdas (`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go:118-176`).
Similar run/update/start container patterns are repeated in other step builders (`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go:188-347`), signaling missing reusable transition primitives.
findings.suggestion: Extract reusable transition primitives (validate alias, mutate config section, container restart policy, publish events) and compose handlers from those primitives so each handler declares intent instead of procedural orchestration.

findings.summary: Session management is implemented as two separate mutable-store patterns with overlapping bucket/list lifecycle logic.
evidence: `ConnectionRegistry` hand-rolls bucket transition, dedupe, stale cleanup, and token registry behavior (`goseg/session/connection_registry.go:106-220`).
`logstreamSessionStore` separately hand-rolls list/map copying, add/remove, and lifecycle mutation operations (`goseg/session/logstream_session_store.go:42-214`).
Both modules repeat nil-guarded mutation APIs and containerized collection management, indicating a missing shared session-store abstraction.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared session collection abstraction (typed bucket operations, dedupe/removal helpers, lifecycle hooks) and adapt both registries to it to centralize invariants and reduce bespoke mutation logic.

findings.summary: Several large test files act as catch-all suites for unrelated behaviors, obscuring design boundaries and ownership.
evidence: `accesspoint_test.go` spans password derivation, config rendering, process probing, shell execution formatting, startup/teardown command orchestration, and failure matrix checks in one file (`goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go:16-940`).

`importer_test.go` combines HTTP validation, token authorization behavior, chunked filesystem IO, orchestration dispatch, lifecycle readiness, and environment preparation in one module (`goseg/importer/importer_test.go:183-936`).

`system/storage_test.go` similarly mixes SMART checks, mount detection, fstab reconciliation, tmpdir linking, filesystem creation, and command behavior (`goseg/system/storage/storage_test.go:49-598`).
findings.suggestion: Split these suites by behavioral slice (validation, filesystem/chunking, orchestration/lifecycle, command plumbing) with per-slice fixtures so tests reflect production boundaries and reduce cognitive load.
